level_1.question: What happens to coastal towns if coral reefs are healthy?
answers: A. Nothing changes
B. Waves hit the shore just as hard
C. Waves are weakened and cause less flooding
D. The ocean disappears

level_1.question: If coral reefs disappear, what could happen to homes and buildings near the coast?
answers: A. They are better protected
B. They could face more flooding damage
C. They become taller
D. They float away

level_1.question: If the ocean becomes more acidic, what happens to baby corals?
answers: A. They grow faster
B. They have trouble building their skeletons
C. They turn into fish
D. They glow in the dark

level_2.question: If corals can’t build strong skeletons, what happens to the reef?
answers: A. The reef gets stronger
B. More sea animals move in
C. The reef breaks down and animals lose their homes
D. The reef moves to a different place

level_2.question: If the Earth warms by 1.5°C, what could happen to coral reefs?
answers: A. Most coral reefs could disappear
B. Coral reefs double in size
C. Coral reefs move to colder planets
D. Nothing happens

level_2.question: Why are coral reefs important for many Pacific Island communities?
answers: A. They provide most of their protein (food)
B. They create electricity
C. They grow trees
D. They make drinking water

level_3.question: If coral reefs decline, how could that affect people?
answers: A. Less seafood and fewer jobs
B. More coral grows
C. Beaches turn purple
D. Fish learn to fly

level_3.question: What happens when unusually warm ocean temperatures cause 'coral bleaching'?
answers: A. The corals are scrubbed clean by fish
B. Corals expel their colorful food-producing algae and can starve
C. The reef turns into white sand
D. The water becomes cloudy and white

level_3.question: How does reducing global carbon emissions help protect coral reefs?
answers: A. It slows down ocean warming and acidification
B. It makes the ocean water saltier
C. It provides the coral with more sunlight
D. It stops storms from forming
